turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has our opinion this morning in case 16-1275, Virginia Uranium v. Warren.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Virginia Uranium, Inc. is a company that seeks to mine a large uranium deposit in the Commonwealth of Virginia. But it faces a problem. Virginia law prohibits mining uranium within state borders. To overcome that obstacle the company filed this lawsuit, arguing that the Federal Atomic Energy Act or AEA preempts Virginia's law under the Supremacy Clause of the United States Constitution. In the AEA, Congress granted the Nuclear Regulatory Commission primary and in some cases exclusive regulatory authority to address nuclear safety concerns in most stages of the nuclear fuel cycle, all the way from initial uranium ore processing, to the storage of spent nuclear fuel. If a state wants to regulate activities within the agency's domain to guard against radiation hazards, it must first obtain an agreement from the NRC. But the company's preemption argument faces a problem. The NRC's regulatory authority takes hold only once nuclear materials are removed from the ground. The Federal Government has no authority to regulate mining itself and the AEA does nothing to withdraw that regulatory power from the states. To be sure the company tries this response, it contends that this Court's precedents, if not the statute, require Courts to inquire, not only into what the state law does and regulates, but why it does so. In the company's view this Court's opinion in Pacific Gas prohibits states from regulating any activity, mining included, if its regulations are based on concerns about nuclear safety. And of course the company says it can prove that Virginia legislators had in mind just such concerns when they enacted their law banning uranium mining. We disagree. We can see nothing in the text or structure of the AEA or even in our precedents that require us to examine Virginia's motives in enacting its laws. The AEA leaves mining regulations to the states, whatever the reasons may be lurking behind them. Our conclusion on this score tracks the Fourth Circuit's judgment which we affirm today. The opinion is joined by Justices Thomas and Kavanaugh. Justice Ginsburg has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment, in which Justices Sotomayor and Kagan have joined. The Chief Justice has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justices Breyer and Alito have joined.
